sources.certVol1: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

sourceAudit.note: Content is original/paraphrased from the cited sources; questions are original. Verify command syntax against official Cisco docs.

objectives.title: Configure and verify inside source NAT using static and pools

ckus.title: NAT
ckus.summary: Network Address Translation maps private (inside local) IPs to public (inside global) IPs so internal hosts can reach the internet.

ckus.title: PAT (NAT Overload)
ckus.summary: Maps many inside addresses to ONE public IP using different source port numbers — the common home/SOHO method.

ckus.title: NAT Inside/Outside Terminology
ckus.summary: Inside local = private host IP; inside global = its translated public IP; outside global = the public destination. Mark interfaces `ip nat inside`/`outside`.

tiers.beginner: Private IP addresses (like 192.168.x.x) can’t travel on the internet, so a router swaps them for a public address on the way out — that’s NAT. At home, one public address is shared by every device using different port numbers; that flavor is called PAT (or “overload”). The router remembers each translation so replies come back to the right device.
tiers.intermediate: NAT translates inside local (private) addresses to inside global (public) addresses. Static NAT is a fixed one-to-one mapping (for servers); dynamic NAT draws from a pool of public addresses; PAT (overload) maps many private hosts to one public IP using unique source ports — the typical SOHO setup. You mark the internal interface `ip nat inside` and the internet-facing one `ip nat outside`, then define what to translate (often an ACL). Verify with `show ip nat translations`.
tiers.examReady: NAT maps inside local ↔ inside global. Types: Static (`ip nat inside source static <local> <global>` — permanent 1:1, for servers); Dynamic (`ip nat pool` + `ip nat inside source list <ACL> pool <name>`); PAT/overload (`ip nat inside source list <ACL> interface <if> overload` — many-to-one via ports). Mark interfaces `ip nat inside` / `ip nat outside`. Terms: inside local (private host), inside global (its public mapping), outside local/global (the destination). Verify: `show ip nat translations`, `show ip nat statistics`.

reading.definition: **NAT** maps private **inside local** IPs to public **inside global** IPs. **PAT (overload)** shares one public IP across many hosts using ports. Interfaces are marked `ip nat inside` / `ip nat outside`.
keyPoints: Static NAT = permanent 1:1 (servers): `ip nat inside source static <local> <global>`.
Dynamic NAT = pool of public addresses.
Mark interfaces `ip nat inside` and `ip nat outside`.
Inside local = private host; inside global = its public address.
Verify: `show ip nat translations`, `show ip nat statistics`.
reading.realWorld: A home router uses PAT so dozens of devices share one ISP address. A web server uses static NAT so it always has the same public IP. Forgetting the `inside`/`outside` interface tags is the #1 reason NAT “does nothing.”
commonMistakes: ACL doesn’t match the intended inside hosts.
Confusing inside local with inside global.
reading.advanced: PAT tracks translations by the tuple of inside global IP + port, allowing ~65k simultaneous sessions per public IP. NAT breaks end-to-end addressing, which is one reason IPv6 (with its huge space) avoids it.

questions.question: What does NAT primarily do?
choices: Maps private IPs to public IPs
questions.explanation: NAT maps inside local (private) to inside global (public) addresses.

questions.question: Many home devices share a single public IP. Which NAT type is this?
questions.explanation: PAT/overload maps many inside hosts to one public IP using ports.

questions.question: Which command creates a permanent 1:1 mapping for a server?
choices: ip nat inside source list 1 interface g0/0 overload
questions.explanation: Static NAT uses `ip nat inside source static <local> <global>`.

questions.question: NAT is configured but nothing is translated. What is most often missing?
choices: The ip nat inside / ip nat outside interface tags
questions.explanation: Interfaces must be marked `ip nat inside` and `ip nat outside`.

questions.question: Which command configures PAT to the outside interface?
choices: ip nat inside source list 1 interface g0/0 overload
questions.explanation: PAT: `ip nat inside source list <ACL> interface <if> overload`.

questions.question: A host’s private address as seen inside the network is called its…
questions.explanation: Inside local = the private address of an inside host.

questions.question: Which command shows active NAT translations?
questions.explanation: `show ip nat translations` lists the current mappings.

questions.question: True or False: PAT distinguishes multiple inside hosts behind one public IP using port numbers.
questions.explanation: True — PAT tracks translations by inside global IP + port.

questions.question: Which method will allow you to use RFC 1918 addresses for Internet requests?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer C. The correct selection is: NAT

questions.question: Using the referenced source exhibit, what is the inside local IP address?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer A. The correct selection is: 192.168.1.2 Host A

questions.question: Using the referenced source exhibit, what is the inside global IP address?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer C. The correct selection is: 179.43.44.1 Router A S0/0

questions.question: Using the referenced source exhibit, what is the outside global IP address?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer D. The correct selection is: 198.23.53.3 web server

questions.question: Which command can view the NAT translations active on the router?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer A. The correct selection is: Router#show ip nat translations

questions.question: Which command display an overview of the current number of active NAT translations on the router, as well as other overview information?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer D. The correct selection is: Router#show ip nat statistics

questions.question: Using the referenced source exhibit, which command will configure static NAT for the internal web server?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer A. The correct selection is: RouterA(config)#ip nat inside source static 192.168.1.3 179.43.44.1

questions.question: Using the referenced source exhibit, the enterprise owns the address block of 179.43.44.0/28. Which command create a NAT pool for Dynamic NAT?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer D. The correct selection is: RouterA(config)#ip nat pool EntPool 179.43.44.2 179.43.44.15 netmask 255.255.255.0

questions.question: When configuring dynamic NAT, why must you configure an access list?
choices: The access list allows incoming access from outside global addresses.
The access list allows outgoing access from inside local addresses.
The access list allows outgoing access from outside local addresses.
The access list allows outgoing access from inside global addresses.
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer B. The correct selection is: The access list allows outgoing access from inside local addresses.

questions.question: Which command wipe out all current NAT translations in the NAT table?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer C. The correct selection is: Router#clear ip nat translation *

questions.question: Which command can see real-time network address translations?
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer B. The correct selection is: Router#debug ip nat

questions.question: Using the referenced source exhibit, which command will configure Port Address Translation?
choices: RouterA(config)#access-list 1 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 RouterA(config)#ip nat pool EntPool 179.43.44.1 179.43.44.1 RouterA(config)#ip nat inside source list 1 pool EntPool
RouterA(config)#ip nat pool EntPool 179.43.44.1 179.43.44.1 netmask 255.255.255.0 RouterA(config)#ip nat source pool EntPool

RouterA(config)#access-list 1 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 RouterA(config)#ip nat pool EntPool 179.43.44.1 179.43.44.1 netmask 255.255.255.0 RouterA(config)#ip nat inside source list 1 pool EntPool overload
RouterA(config)#access-list 1 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 RouterA(config)#ip nat pool EntPool 179.43.44.1 179.43.44.1 netmask 255.255.255.0 RouterA(config)#ip nat inside source list 1 pool EntPool
questions.explanation: The source maps this item to answer C. The correct selection is: RouterA(config)#access-list 1 permit 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 RouterA(config)#ip nat pool EntPool 179.43.44.1 179.43.44.1 netmask 255.255.255.0 RouterA(config)#ip nat inside source list 1 pool EntPool...

flashcards.back: Many inside hosts → one public IP using unique source ports.

flashcards.front: PAT command to the outside interface?
flashcards.back: ip nat inside source list <ACL> interface <if> overload.
ip nat inside (private side) and ip nat outside (public side).
show ip nat translations; show ip nat statistics.

commands.purpose: Create a permanent one-to-one NAT mapping.
commands.example: R1(config)# ip nat inside source static 192.168.1.10 203.0.113.10

commands.command: ip nat inside source list <acl> interface <if> overload
commands.purpose: Configure PAT (overload) to the outside interface.
commands.example: R1(config)# ip nat inside source list 1 interface g0/0 overload

glossary.definition: Network Address Translation — maps private to public IP addresses.
Port Address Translation (NAT overload) — many hosts share one public IP via ports.
The private IP of an inside host.
The public IP an inside host is translated to.

mnemonics.title: Overload = ports
mnemonics.mnemonic: Overload shares one IP with many ports.
mnemonics.explanation: PAT multiplexes hosts behind one public IP using source ports.

examTraps.correction: Without `ip nat inside`/`outside`, NAT translates nothing.

examTraps.trap: Mixing up inside local and inside global.
examTraps.correction: Inside local = private host; inside global = its public mapping.

examTraps.trap: Expecting static NAT to serve many hosts.
examTraps.correction: Static NAT is 1:1; use PAT/overload for many-to-one.

misconceptions.reality: NAT hides addresses but does not encrypt; it is not a security control by itself.
misconceptions.example: Traffic is still cleartext after translation.

misconceptions.misconception: Every inside host needs its own public IP.
misconceptions.reality: PAT lets thousands of hosts share one public IP via ports.
misconceptions.example: A home network uses a single ISP address.

diagram.title: PAT shares one public IP
annotations: Both hosts share 203.0.113.1, distinguished by source port.
Inside local → inside global translation tracked per port.

steps.title: Packet out
steps.action: Inside host sends to the internet with its private source IP.

steps.title: Translate
steps.action: Router rewrites the source to the public IP + a unique port; records the mapping.

steps.title: Reply
steps.action: Return traffic hits the public IP + port.

steps.title: Reverse
steps.action: Router maps it back to the original inside host.

lab.title: Configure NAT Overload (PAT) for Internet Access
lab.scenario: R1 connects a private LAN (192.168.1.0/24) to the internet via its Gi0/0 outside interface (203.0.113.1/30). You will mark the inside/outside interfaces, define which inside addresses are allowed to be translated, and configure PAT so every inside host shares the single outside IP.
learningGoals: Mark interfaces as NAT inside vs outside.
Write a standard ACL describing the inside addresses to translate.
Configure NAT overload (PAT) referencing the ACL and outside interface.
Verify active translations with show ip nat translations.

tasks.title: Addressing
tasks.instruction: Configure Gi0/1 (inside) = 192.168.1.1/24 and Gi0/0 (outside) = 203.0.113.1/30. Bring both up.

tasks.title: Mark inside/outside
tasks.instruction: Mark Gi0/1 as the NAT inside interface and Gi0/0 as the NAT outside interface.

tasks.title: Define inside addresses
tasks.instruction: Create standard ACL 1 permitting the 192.168.1.0/24 network — this defines which addresses get translated.

tasks.title: Enable PAT
tasks.instruction: Configure NAT overload (PAT) so ACL 1 traffic is translated to the Gi0/0 outside IP with port-level multiplexing.
expectedCommands: ip nat inside source list 1 interface gi0/0 overload

tasks.title: Generate and verify traffic
tasks.instruction: From an inside host (192.168.1.10), ping an outside address, then verify the translation table on R1.

successCriteria: show ip nat translations shows 192.168.1.10:xxxxx translated to 203.0.113.1:xxxxx after the ping.
The outside host receives traffic only from 203.0.113.1 — never sees the 192.168.1.x address.
Multiple inside hosts can be active at once, each using a different source port on 203.0.113.1.
show ip nat statistics shows hits incrementing for the overload translation.
failureCriteria: ACL written too narrow (e.g. host instead of /24) → only one inside host can be translated.
Interfaces not marked inside/outside → "translation failed" — no inside/outside interfaces.
Using `ip nat inside source static` instead of `list ... overload` → only one-to-one mapping, no port sharing.
ACL with an implicit deny that blocks the real inside subnet → traffic not translated, dropped outbound.
commonMistakes: Forgetting `overload`, which limits NAT to one inside host per outside IP (no PAT).
Marking both interfaces as "inside" or both as "outside".
Writing the ACL wildcard mask backwards (e.g. 255.255.255.0 instead of 0.0.0.255).
Expecting `show ip nat translations` to show entries before any traffic has been sent.

topology.title: PAT topology
notes: All inside hosts share the single outside address 203.0.113.1, distinguished by source port.

requiredCommands.command: ip nat inside source list 1 interface gi0/0 overload

verificationChecks.expectedResult: Hits counter > 0 for the dynamic overload translation.

failureChecks.expectedFailure: No entries after a ping from PC1
failureChecks.reason: Missing `ip nat inside`/`outside` markers or a too-narrow ACL prevents translation.

diagram.title: PAT translation table
annotations: Both hosts share 203.0.113.1, distinguished by source port.
Inside local -> inside global translation tracked per port.

steps.title: Translate
steps.action: R1 rewrites the source to 203.0.113.1:40001 and records the mapping.

steps.title: Reverse
steps.action: R1 maps the reply back to 192.168.1.10:1500 and forwards it to PC1.

lab.title: Configure SSH for Secure Remote Device Access
lab.scenario: R1 is currently only manageable via the console. You will configure a hostname and domain name (required for RSA key generation), generate an SSH key pair, create a local administrator account, and restrict the VTY lines to SSH-only with local login — then verify an admin PC can SSH in but Telnet is refused.
learningGoals: Explain why a hostname and domain name are prerequisites for `crypto key generate rsa`.
Generate an RSA key pair to enable SSH.
Create a local user account and require local authentication on the VTY lines.
Restrict VTY access to SSH only (`transport input ssh`).

tasks.title: Hostname and domain
tasks.instruction: Set the hostname to R1 and the domain name to ccna.local (required before generating RSA keys).

tasks.title: Generate RSA keys
tasks.instruction: Generate a 1024-bit RSA key pair, which automatically enables SSH.

tasks.title: Local admin account
tasks.instruction: Create a local user "admin" with privilege level 15 and a secret password.

tasks.title: Restrict VTY to SSH
tasks.instruction: On the VTY lines (0 4), require local login and restrict the transport protocol to SSH only.

tasks.title: Verify from the admin PC
tasks.instruction: SSH to R1 using the admin account, and confirm Telnet is refused.

successCriteria: show ip ssh shows SSH enabled (version 1.99 or 2) with an active RSA key.
PC1 can SSH to R1 and authenticate with the local admin account.
Attempting Telnet to R1 from PC1 is refused (transport input ssh only).
show running-config | section line vty shows `login local` and `transport input ssh`.
failureCriteria: No domain name set before `crypto key generate rsa` → the command is rejected (no keys generated, SSH stays disabled).
VTY lines left at `login` (no `local`) with no password set → access denied or "password required but none set" error.
`transport input` left at default (all/telnet) → Telnet still works, defeating the purpose.
Local user created without `privilege 15` → user can log in but cannot reach privileged EXEC without the enable secret.
commonMistakes: Forgetting `ip domain-name` before generating RSA keys — the crypto command silently fails without it.
Using `login` instead of `login local` on the VTY lines — `login` alone expects a line password, not a username/password.
Leaving `transport input telnet ssh` (the default) instead of restricting to `ssh` only.
Setting the user password with `password` instead of `secret` — `secret` is hashed, `password` is reversible.

topology.title: SSH management topology
notes: VTY 0 4 configured with `login local` + `transport input ssh`.

verificationChecks.expectedResult: SSH session opens, prompts for the admin secret.

failureChecks.reason: `transport input ssh` removes Telnet access on the VTY lines.

diagram.title: SSH allowed, Telnet refused on VTY
annotations: `transport input ssh` on VTY 0 4 permits only SSH.
`login local` requires the locally configured username/secret.

steps.title: SSH request
steps.action: PC1 opens an SSH session to R1 on TCP/22.

steps.title: VTY accepts
steps.action: VTY 0 4 has `transport input ssh` — the SSH session is accepted.

steps.title: Local login
steps.action: `login local` prompts for the admin username/secret and authenticates against the local database.

steps.title: Privileged access
steps.action: Because admin has privilege 15, the session starts in privileged EXEC.

steps.title: Telnet request
steps.action: PC1 attempts a Telnet session to R1 on TCP/23.

steps.title: Transport check
steps.action: VTY 0 4 only allows `transport input ssh` — Telnet (TCP/23) is not permitted.

steps.title: Connection refused
steps.action: The router refuses the Telnet connection.
